title: Second Law & Entropy
subtitle: Second Law of Thermodynamics for Class 11 Physics — entropy, irreversible processes

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
The Second Law of Thermodynamics places a fundamental direction on natural processes:
Kelvin–Planck statement: No heat engine can convert heat entirely into work in a cycle — some heat must always be rejected to a cold reservoir.
Clausius statement: Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body without external work.
Both are equivalent and both say: entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
Entropy (S): A measure of disorder or the number of microscopic states available to the system. The universe tends toward maximum entropy — things fall apart, mix, and equalise.

blocks.heading: Entropy change:
blocks.body:
For a reversible isothermal process:
Second Law:
Equality holds for reversible processes; strict inequality for all real (irreversible) processes.
Efficiency limit:
No engine can be more efficient than the Carnot efficiency:

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Refrigerator: A refrigerator moves heat from cold (inside) to hot (room). This is not spontaneous — it requires work (electrical energy). The second law is not violated because we are pumping in external work. The total entropy of the universe still increases: the entropy decrease inside the fridge is more than compensated by entropy increase of the surroundings (from the electrical work).

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Mix hot water (70°C) with cold water (10°C) and measure the final temperature. Calculate ΔS for the hot water (loses heat), ΔS for the cold water (gains heat). Add them up — the total ΔS should be positive (entropy increased). This is irreversible mixing.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Could a Maxwell's Demon — an intelligent being that can sort fast and slow molecules — violate the second law? Modern physics says no: the demon must store information to sort the molecules, and erasing that information costs entropy. The act of measurement and information erasure generates at least as much entropy as would be reduced. Information has an entropy cost.

quiz.question: For a reversible process in an isolated system, the entropy change is:
quiz.explanation: For a reversible process ΔS = 0 (entropy is conserved). Irreversible processes always have ΔS > 0. ΔS < 0 is forbidden for isolated systems.
